title: Towards elementary infinity-toposes
speaker: Michael Shulman
abstract: Abstract: Toposes were invented by Grothendieck to abstract properties of categories of sheaves, but soon Lawvere and Tierney realized that the elementary (i.e. "finitary" or first-order) properties satisfied by Grothendieck's toposes were precisely those characterizing a "generalized category of sets". An elementary topos shares most of the properties of Grothendieck's, as well as supporting an "internal language" that enables it to be used as a basis for mathematics.

proof_stages.label: Coherent equivalence as Voevodsky’s key contribution
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates elementary infinity-toposes by tracing how homotopy interpretations of type theory made naive isomorphism data inadequate and how Voevodsky’s definitions of contractibility, h-levels, and equivalence give finitary, proposition-valued notions that correctly see higher structure.

proof_stages.label: Elementary toposes as finitary internal set theories
proof_stages.summary: Ordinary elementary toposes are presented as categories with finitary set-like structure—finite limits and colimits, exponentials, and a subobject classifier—whose slice stability yields local Cartesian closure and the categorical interpretation of dependent sums and products.

proof_stages.label: From subobject classifiers to object classifiers
proof_stages.summary: The argument upgrades the subobject-classifier idea from subsets to arbitrary families, observes that isomorphism classes lose automorphism data, and thereby explains why object classifiers naturally live in an infinity-one categorical setting, as in the Lurie–Rezk characterization of Grothendieck infinity-toposes.

proof_stages.label: Finitary axioms for elementary infinity-toposes
proof_stages.summary: A candidate elementary infinity-topos is extracted by retaining finite homotopy limits and colimits, local Cartesian closure, a subobject classifier, and enough universe-like object classifiers closed under the relevant type-forming operations.

proof_stages.label: Infinitary coherence obstruction
proof_stages.summary: The speaker explains why apparently finite categorical constructions such as idempotent splittings, quotients, images, and even the definition of an infinity-one categorical universal property can require infinite coherent data, making object classifiers the hardest finitary axiom to formulate.

proof_stages.label: Finitary univalence via the equivalence classifier
proof_stages.summary: Using local Cartesian closure and Voevodsky’s contractible-fiber definition of equivalence, one constructs an internal classifier for equivalences and expresses an object classifier’s univalence finitarily as the equivalence between the diagonal/path object and this equivalence object.

proof_stages.label: Natural numbers object and derived infinitary constructions
proof_stages.summary: The proposed axioms are shown, at least in type-theoretic form, to imply a natural numbers object by extracting rigid finite ordered objects from a universe, and that object then supports constructions such as idempotent splittings, images, quotients, truncations, and related closure properties.

proof_stages.label: Internal language, initiality, and open problems
proof_stages.summary: Because many claimed theorems are proved in type theory rather than directly in infinity-categorical language, the talk identifies the needed initiality/soundness and strictification results and ends with open problems about syntax, non-Grothendieck examples, recursive higher inductive types, and logical functors.

examples.name: Naive homotopy inverse of the identity as a loop
examples.note: Specializing the old isomorphism data to the identity map reveals an unwanted loop of homotopical data, explaining why a coherent definition of equivalence is needed.

examples.name: Dedekind reals yielding continuous real-valued functions
examples.note: Defining reals internally as Dedekind cuts in a sheaf topos concretely shows why power objects and internal subsets are geometrically meaningful.
